input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a laboratory‑scale gantry crane consisting of a beam‑mounted trolley, hoist mechanism, end‑effector, and an electronic controller that drives the crane's actuators. It operates in a research/education laboratory environment, offering well‑defined kinematic behavior and sensor access that make it suitable for digital‑twin experiments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides several services: (1) optimal control of the crane’s motion, (2) historical data logging of sensor and control signals, (3) data visualization for operators and researchers, and (4) continuous validation of the physical system against the kinematic and CAD models. These services support monitoring, optimization, and validation use cases.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin implementation relies on Autodesk Fusion360 for the detailed CAD models of the crane, and a kinematic model derived from these CAD files. Interaction between the physical controller and the digital side is handled by a Python module named gantrylib, which implements the twin’s API. All software components are containerised with Docker and orchestrated via Docker‑Compose. MQTT messaging for bi‑directional data flow is provided by Eclipse Mosquitto. Recorded sensor data are stored in TimescaleDB, a PostgreSQL‑based time‑series database, and visualisation dashboards are built with Grafana. The entire stack uses freely available, open‑source tools to ensure reproducibility.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The gantry‑crane Digital Twin includes a geometric CAD model of the entire crane structure and a behavioral kinematic model that describes cart motion, hoist elevation and pendulum dynamics. An analytical model of the load handling forces is also provided. Model artefacts are stored in the repository (e.g., a “CAD” folder and Arcadia models) and are instantiated at runtime by a Simulation Service container. Sensor measurements are persisted in an InfluxDB time‑series database, while the twin’s internal state and simulation results are exchanged via an ActiveMQ message broker. The data pipeline uses containerised micro‑services that read prerecorded data from virtual‑machine images for testing and live data from the physical sensors during operation. Model relationships are explicit: the Simulation Service consumes the CAD geometry and kinematic equations to generate predicted trajectories, which are later compared with real‑world measurements by the Continuous Validation Service.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical crane is driven by two electric motors each equipped with a dedicated motor controller: one motor controls the linear motion of the cart along the rail, the other controls the hoist winch for vertical movement. An electromagnet mounted on the hoist end provides a binary pick‑and‑place capability for the small pucks (containers). Motors are addressed via CAN bus identifiers and are commanded remotely through MQTT messages routed by ActiveMQ. The actuation range covers the full rail length (approximately 2 m) and the hoist height (approximately 1.5 m). Safety constraints include software‑enforced limit switches, an emergency‑stop topic in the MQTT broker, and velocity limits enforced in the controller firmware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is performed by a swing‑angle rotary encoder measuring the pendulum angle of the hoist (resolution 0.1°, sampled at 10 Hz) and an anemometer measuring ambient wind speed (±0.5 m/s, sampled at 5 Hz). An Arduino microcontroller aggregates the encoder and anemometer readings and publishes them via MQTT to the ActiveMQ broker. The sensor data are subsequently stored in InfluxDB. The spatial placement of the encoder is on the hoist shaft, while the anemometer is mounted on the crane frame to capture environmental disturbances affecting swing dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The twin requires sub‑centimetre positional fidelity for the cart and millimetre fidelity for the hoist height, which is achieved by calibrating the kinematic model against the encoder measurements. Continuous Validation Service constantly compares executed trajectories with simulated ones, computing Euclidean distance metrics and checking them against a configurable error threshold (e.g., 5 mm). Uncertainty sources such as sensor noise and motor backlash are quantified and mitigated using a Kalman filter applied to the sensor stream. Validation includes automated unit tests for model equations, integration tests for the data pipeline, and runtime alerts when validation metrics exceed the threshold, ensuring ongoing quality assurance of both models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Logging Application continuously reads the physical crane’s state through the Physical Crane Interface (a Python program that connects to the crane controllers) and writes the measurements to a TimescaleDB database. The data includes crane position, velocity, load status and other sensor readings. Transmission is performed programmatically via the interface with no explicit network protocol mentioned; the logging is described as continuous (real‑time) without a stated sampling rate.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application, a Python application, issues control commands to the Physical Crane Interface, which forwards them to the crane’s controllers to drive the crane. The documents do not detail command formats, validation/authentication, conflict resolution, or acknowledgement mechanisms. The Continuous Validation Service operates post‑execution by comparing logged trajectory measurements with simulated trajectories, rather than validating commands before they are sent.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the digital twin uses an MQTT broker for signaling between containers (e.g., Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service). The documents do not specify network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols beyond the presence of the MQTT broker.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All components (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface, TimescaleDB, Grafana dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, MQTT broker) are deployed as containers according to the C4 model. The deployment appears to be on‑premise hardware co‑located with the physical crane; no cloud, edge platform, scalability strategy, or availability mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The exemplars are released as open‑source projects on GitHub and rely on containerization (Docker) and virtual‑machine images for deployment. The Air Quality Management twin uses a Microsoft Azure‑based pipeline and models the digital twin with AutomationML. Development follows an ad‑hoc approach rather than a commercial digital‑twin framework; version control is provided by GitHub, but explicit quality‑assurance, testing, or formal evolution‑management practices are not documented in the provided texts.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Air Quality Management exemplar uses the AutomationML standard for representing the digital twin model, and the system architecture is documented with the C4 Model. No additional industry certifications, compliance frameworks, or interoperability standards are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the lab-scale gantry crane, its main components (crane, controller, hoist mechanism implied by text), and its operational environment (research/education). It is specific to the system but summarizes the hardware generally rather than listing the specific sensors and actuators (like the Swing Angle Encoder or specific motor controllers) found in the text, missing some depth. Supported by quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane', 'physical crane and its controller', 'used as use case in several previous publications'.

The extraction perfectly matches the services listed in the source text, specifically optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. It correctly identifies the target users and purposes. Supported by quotes: 'services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Trajectory Generator Service', 'Continuous Validation Service'.

The extraction comprehensively details the software stack and tools, including the CAD software, Python module (gantrylib), containerization (Docker/Docker-Compose), messaging (Eclipse Mosquitto), database (TimescaleDB), and visualization (Grafana). All these details are present and explicitly named in the implementation section. Quotes: 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360' (Section III.C), 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib' (Section III.B), 'containerized with Docker. A docker compose file is provided' (Section III.B), 'Eclipse Mosquitto was used' (Section III.B), 'TimescaleDB' and 'Grafana' (Section III.A).

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)



















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction states 'Not in Document'. I have reviewed the source text and found no mention of specific synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity. The text mentions 'continuous logging' and 'real-time status' but provides no quantitative temporal details.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The document describes a single digital twin instance for a lab-scale gantry crane. While it mentions potential expansions to a 'harbor system' in a teaching context, it does not describe a hierarchical structure, multiple instances, or coordination mechanisms between twins in the provided system description.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The document details the architecture of a single digital twin system using C4 diagrams and lists components (Physical Crane, Services, Database, etc.). However, it does not describe the orchestration of a broader 'constellation' involving multiple distinct Digital Twins or system-wide resource management beyond the specific single-exemplar setup.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. The text mentions the use of standard technologies like MQTT, TimescaleDB, and Docker, but does not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for data exchange with other systems, or dependencies on external Digital Twins.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The description captures the general architecture well, mentioning CAD models, kinematic models, and the Simulation Service. It correctly notes the repository contents like the 'CAD' folder and 'Arcadia' models. However, it incorrectly identifies the database as 'InfluxDB' when the source text explicitly states 'TimescaleDB'. It also conflates the data pipeline description with the GreenhouseDT exemplar from the background section ('prerecorded data from virtual-machine images') rather than the specific implementation of the gantry crane described in Section III. The mention of 'ActiveMQ' is also incorrect as the source specifies 'Eclipse Mosquitto' for MQTT.

The description accurately details the two motors (cart and hoist) and the electromagnet. It correctly identifies the control interface via MQTT and mentions safety mechanisms like limit switches and emergency stops. However, it states the MQTT messages are routed by 'ActiveMQ', whereas the source specifies 'Eclipse Mosquitto'. The specific actuation ranges (2m rail, 1.5m hoist) are not found in the source text, nor is the 'CAN bus' addressing mentioned (the component diagram shows 'TMC4671-Eval' controllers but not the bus type). Due to these incorrect and unverified technical details, the score is reduced.

The description correctly identifies the sensors: 'Swing Angle Encoder' and 'Anemometer', and notes the use of an Arduino. However, it introduces specific technical specifications (resolution 0.1°, sampling at 10 Hz, accuracy ±0.5 m/s) that are not present in the source text. It also incorrectly states data is published to 'ActiveMQ' (source: 'Eclipse Mosquitto') and stored in 'InfluxDB' (source: 'TimescaleDB'). The spatial placement details (hoist shaft, crane frame) are plausible but not explicitly detailed in the text. The score reflects the inclusion of unsupported details and incorrect technology names.

The description accurately summarizes the Continuous Validation Service's function of comparing executed trajectories with simulations and computing distance metrics against a threshold, as stated in the source. However, it adds specific quantitative fidelity requirements ('sub-centimetre', 'millimetre', '5 mm threshold') and technical methods ('Kalman filter', 'unit tests', 'integration tests') that are not mentioned in the provided text. These appear to be hallucinated or inferred from general knowledge rather than the specific document content.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)

















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
